Evaluate the following student answer based on the rubric.

Question: Explain Continuous Deployment workflow with a neat diagram.
Ideal Rubric: Continuous deployment is an extension of CD, but this time, with a process that
automates the entire CI/CD pipeline from the moment the developer commits their
code to deployment in production through all of the verification steps.
This practice is rarely implemented in enterprises because it requires a variety of tests (unit, functional, integration, performance, and so on) to be covered for the application.
Successfully executing these tests is sufficient to validate the proper functionality of
the application regarding all of these dependencies. However, it also allows you to
automatically deploy to a production environment without any approval action required.
The continuous deployment process must also take into account all of the steps to restore the application in the event of a production problem.
Continuous deployment can be implemented by using and implementing feature toggle techniques (or feature flags), which involves encapsulating the application's functionalities in features and activating its features on demand, directly in production, without having to redeploy the code of the application.
Another technique is to use a blue-green production infrastructure, which consists
of two production environments, one blue and one green. First, we deploy to the blue environment, then to the green one; this will ensure that no downtime is required.

Max Marks: 7
Student Answer (OCR Text): 1  
Module - 1  

1(a)  
Continuous Integration is a part of the CI-CD pipeline.  
It is used to integrate the code into the process for the further processing.  
• The code is pushed by the developer to the Source Code Manager that manages the programs.  
• The Build is also present in the CI phase.  
• The code is pushed into a platform like git, TravisCI.  

[Diagram]  
- A person icon labeled "code" pointing to a diamond shape labeled "Source Code Manager".  
- An arrow from the diamond shape points to a rectangle labeled "CI Server".  
- Inside the rectangle, there are two smaller rectangles labeled "Build" and "Test".  
- An arrow exits the "Test" rectangle and loops back to the person icon.  

• The CI server consists of the Build and Test.  
• The code that is sent is first present in the SCM, that accumulates the data for processing.  
• The Build does and compiles the code for the further testing.  
• Agile methods are used for continuous integration as it is more efficient and helps test each part for error.

---

- The test part of the Continuous Integration pipeline does 2 parts.  
- Unit testing:  
  - Each unit of the code that is sent at the current stage is processed and tested for any errors.  
  - This helps by testing each part we reduce the chances of the occurrence of an error in the further states.  

- Integration testing:  
  - The components are all integrated together and also tested for any errors or functionality checking.  
  - These tests are done to check if it can be sent further to the other steps.  
  - If there are no problems encountered it is further sent for processing.  
  - If any changes are required, it sends it back to the developer for changes.

text_input:
Evaluate the following student answer based on the rubric.

Question: Apply IaC practices for scripting type using Azure CLI, PowerShell and declarative type for starting nginx server.
Ideal Rubric: 
These are scripts such as Bash, PowerShell, or others that use the different clients (SDKs) provided by the cloud provider; for example, you can script the provisioning of an Azure infrastructure with the Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell.

az group create --location westeurope --resource-group
MyAppResourcegroup

Using Azure PowerShell we have the following:
New-AzResourceGroup -Name MyAppResourcegroup -Location
Westeurope

The problem with these languages and tools is that they require a lot of lines of code. This is because we need to manage the different states of the manipulated resources, and it is necessary to write all the steps of creating or updating the desired infrastructure.

Declarative types
These are languages in which it is sufficient to write the state of the desired system or infrastructure in the form of configuration and properties. This is the case, for example, for Terraform and Vagrant from HashiCorp, Ansible, the Azure ARM template, Azure Bicep  PowerShell DSC, Puppet, and Chef.
For example, the following Terraform code allows you to define the desired configuration of an Azure resource group:

resource "azurerm_resource_group" "myrg" {
name = "MyAppResourceGroup"
location = "West Europe"
tags = {
environment = "Bookdemo"
}
}

Here is another example that allows you to install and restart nginx on a server
using Ansible:

---
- hosts: all
tasks:
- name: install and check nginx latest version
apt: name=nginx state=latest
- name: start nginx
service:
name: nginx
state: started

Max Marks: 8

Question: Apply IaC practices for scripting type using Azure CLI, PowerShell and declarative type for starting nginx server.
Ideal Rubric: 
These are scripts such as Bash, PowerShell, or others that use the different clients (SDKs) provided by the cloud provider; for example, you can script the provisioning of an Azure infrastructure with the Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell.

az group create --location westeurope --resource-group
MyAppResourcegroup

Using Azure PowerShell we have the following:
New-AzResourceGroup -Name MyAppResourcegroup -Location
Westeurope

The problem with these languages and tools is that they require a lot of lines of code. This is because we need to manage the different states of the manipulated resources, and it is necessary to write all the steps of creating or updating the desired infrastructure.

Declarative types
These are languages in which it is sufficient to write the state of the desired system or infrastructure in the form of configuration and properties. This is the case, for example, for Terraform and Vagrant from HashiCorp, Ansible, the Azure ARM template, Azure Bicep  PowerShell DSC, Puppet, and Chef.
For example, the following Terraform code allows you to define the desired configuration of an Azure resource group:

resource "azurerm_resource_group" "myrg" {
name = "MyAppResourceGroup"
location = "West Europe"
tags = {
environment = "Bookdemo"
}
}

Question: Explain following git commands with syntax and an example.
1.Push 2. merge 3. commit 4. Checkout
Ideal Rubric: 
1. git push
Purpose:
Uploads (pushes) your local commits to a remote repository (like GitHub, GitLab, etc.) so others can access your latest work.
Syntax:
git push <remote_name> <branch_name>
•	remote_name → usually origin (the default name of the remote repo)
•	branch_name → usually main or master, but it could be any branch
Example:
git push origin main
2. git merge
Purpose:
Combines changes from one branch into another — commonly used when you finish a feature branch and want to merge it into the main branch.
Syntax:
git merge <branch_name>

3. git commit
Purpose:
Records the staged changes (those added using git add) into the local repository with a descriptive message.
Syntax:
git commit -m "commit message"
•	-m → specifies the commit message inline
•	The message should briefly describe what you changed
4. git checkout
Purpose:
Used to switch between branches or restore files to a previous state.
Syntax (to switch branch):
git checkout <branch_name>
Syntax (to create and switch to a new branch):
git checkout -b <new_branch_name>

Question: Illustrate the steps involved in Understanding the Git process and Gitflow
Ideal Rubric: 
 
1. The first developer commits the code on the local repository and pushes it to the remote repository
•	The local repository is the developer’s working copy on their own computer.
•	The developer makes changes (adds new files or modifies existing ones).
2. The second developer gets the pushed code from the remote repository
•	The remote repository now contains the latest version of the project.
•	The second developer needs to make sure they have the same version before starting any new work.
•	
  3. The second developer updates this code, creates a commit, and pushes the new version of the code to the remote repository
•	Now the second developer works on the latest version of the code — perhaps fixing a bug, improving performance, or adding a new feature.
4. Finally, the first developer retrieves the latest version of the code in the local repository
•	The first developer’s local copy is now out of date since the second developer has pushed new changes.
•	To update it, the first developer must pull the latest code from the remote repository

Ideal Rubric: 
In order for Jenkins to run a new job, we must first create a webhook in the GitHub
repository. This webhook will be used to notify Jenkins as soon as a new push occurs in the repository.
To do this, follow these steps:

1. In the GitHub repository, go to the Settings | Webhooks menu.
2. Click on the Add Webhook button.
3. In the Payload URL field, fill in the URL address of Jenkins followed by /githubwebhook/, leave the secret input as it is, and choose the Just the push event
option.

1.	 Payload URL is the endpoint (web address) where GitHub sends a POST request whenever an event (like a code push) happens.
2.	This payload contains a JSON message with all the details about the event — who made the change, which branch was updated, commit details, etc.
3.	Jenkins receives this payload and decides whether to trigger a build job based on the configuration.
he general format is:
http://<JENKINS_SERVER_URL>:<PORT>/github-webhook/
or if Jenkins uses HTTPS:
https://<JENKINS_SERVER_URL>/github-webhook/

4. Validate the webhook.
